title: Machaeranthera boltoniae
taxonomy: Machaeranthera boltoniae is classified within the family Asteraceae, which is commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This plant belongs to the tribe Astereae and is further classified under the subfamily Asteroideae. Within the genus Machaeranthera, M. boltoniae is a species of flowering plant native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The classification is based on botanical characteristics and molecular analysis. The exact classification hierarchy is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Asterids, Order: Asterales, Family: Asteraceae, Tribe: Astereae, Genus: Machaeranthera, Species: M. boltoniae.
uses: Machaeranthera boltoniae, also known as Bolton's greenthread or Bolton's threadplant, is a plant with potential medicinal uses. Although its traditional uses are not well-documented, it has been used by some indigenous cultures for various health benefits. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, which could be beneficial for wound healing and skin issues. Further research is needed to fully understand its potential as a medicinal plant. Traditional herbalists recommend using the plant's roots and leaves, but it's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any plant for medicinal purposes.

intro: Machaeranthera boltoniae, also known as Bolton's tansy aster, is a perennial plant species belonging to the aster family (Asteraceae). Traditionally, the plant is used to treat various health issues due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of digestive issues, wounds, and skin irritations. The bioactive constituents of Machaeranthera boltoniae include sesquiterpene lactones, particularly echinulin and leucodine, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is often used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, although its use should be approached with caution due to potential toxicity.
